action.reasoning: Observation: val_eval.py hit persistent CUDA OOM (second process holding ~47GB VRAM causing fragmentation on offset 270 tests) leading to inf LPIPS and useless best_offset=0; custom per-target rays (rel_az + offset, validated renderer call model.renderer(model.decoder, triplane, rays_o, rays_d), robust output/rgba handling, white-BG composite) previously achieved stable val runs but LPIPS~0.61 (vs best 0.6021 from partial orbit fallback). Workspace has working get_spherical_cameras_with_azimuth, get_rgb_from_output, tensor_to_rgba_pil, compute_metrics. Analysis: TripoSR assumes canonical input pose (common offset ~180° needed as dataset input_az varies widely); OOM from large chunk_size=131072 + no per-view cleanup in sweep. Smaller chunk=16384, explicit torch.cuda.empty_cache()/gc.collect() after EVERY view, limit=5 objs + 5 offsets, per-obj try/except, model.load once, PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF, and input.convert('RGB') + white-bg composite will yield reliable best_offset (expect LPIPS<0.55 with good offset). Zero-shot TripoSR+pose alignment remains optimal (<1B params, no training). Decision: Fully rewrite val_eval.py (complete self-contained low-mem version with limit, cleanup, error resilience) and predict.py (reads best_offset.txt, applies offset in rel_az=(target_az-input_az+offset)%360, same robust funcs/low chunk, per-obj try/except+gc, meets exact eval contract with RGBA 256x256 saves + JSON). Run val_eval.py to update best_offset.txt with good value + print metrics, then submit_eval on updated predict.py for improved LPIPS.
